diagrams.title: Union Construction (ε-NFA)
diagrams.description: Shows how to combine two NFAs for union operation
elements: New start state S with ε-transitions to N1 start and N2 start
Both NFA1 and NFA2 maintain their original accepting states
Single accepting state with ε-transitions from both original accepting states
diagrams.purpose: Visualize how parallel acceptance works in union closure proof

diagrams.title: Product Construction for Intersection
diagrams.description: DFA state diagram showing product automaton
elements: Grid of states (p,q) where p from M1 and q from M2
Transition from (p,q) on a goes to (δ1(p,a), δ2(q,a))
Accepting states marked where both p and q are accepting in original DFAs
diagrams.purpose: Understanding how intersection is computed by simultaneous simulation

diagrams.title: Complement Construction
diagrams.description: DFA before and after complement operation
elements: Original DFA with some accepting states
Complement DFA with all accepting/non-accepting states swapped
diagrams.purpose: Visualize how complement is obtained by state flipping

diagrams.title: Kleene Star Construction
diagrams.description: NFA for L* showing loop structure
elements: New start state that is accepting
ε-loop back to original start from all accepting states
Original NFA connected to new start
diagrams.purpose: Visualize how repetition (zero or more) is achieved

diagrams.title: Concatenation Construction
elements: NFA for L1 with accepting states
ε-transitions from each accepting state of L1 to start of L2
No new start needed - use L1's start
diagrams.purpose: Visualize how strings from L1 followed by L2 are accepted

diagrams.title: Reversal Construction
diagrams.description: NFA for L^R showing reversed transitions
elements: Original accepting states become start states
New single start state with ε to all original acceptors
diagrams.purpose: Visualize how reversal is achieved by reversing flow

visuals.title: Closure Properties Of Regular Languages
visuals.description: Visual diagram for closure properties of regular languages
